main_damage_causes: The complete absence of Wood's defining comic arc in PoA — the dawn training session, the Firebolt/McGonagall disaster, and above all the Quidditch Cup win with his tearful breakdown — removes the emotional payoff of his three-book character journey

Systematic compression of Wood's personality in PS/CoS films: his manic pre-match intensity, competitive fury at Flint, and obsessive tactical preparation are replaced by brief functional appearances, leaving him a named face rather than a vivid character

The invented 'Bludger to the head' anecdote and Wood being knocked out mid-match in PS replace book-accurate characterisation with softer, more generic beats that misrepresent his voice and competence

damaging_scenes.scene: Film Scene 4 / Film Scene 5 — Seeker recruitment and Quidditch rules explanation
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Wood reacts with pure delight and manic enthusiasm when told Harry is a Seeker. He walks around Harry assessing his build, immediately talks brooms and strategy, and his obsessive passion for Quidditch is fully on display. The rules explanation scene is a detailed, characterful exchange showing Wood's expertise, his slight obliviousness to Muggle references, and his genuine investment in Harry as a player.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: The recruitment scene is compressed to a single line ('Potter, this is Oliver Wood. Wood — I have found you a Seeker!'). Wood has no spoken reaction of delight, no assessment of Harry's build, no broom discussion. The rules explanation is reduced to a brief functional summary with almost no personality or back-and-forth.
damaging_scenes.explanation: Wood's defining trait — obsessive, almost unhinged enthusiasm for Quidditch — is established in these scenes in the books. The film strips the characterisation to a functional cameo, leaving him a named face rather than a personality.

damaging_scenes.scene: Film Scene 8 — Pre-match locker room speech (PS film, Gryffindor vs Slytherin)
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Wood gives a speech the Weasley twins know by heart, glares at the team with implicit threat, and delivers 'We're going to win. I know it.' with fierce conviction. The scene establishes his intensity and the team's affectionate exasperation with him.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: The locker room exchange is replaced by a brief exchange outside ('Scared, Harry?' / 'A little.' / Wood's anecdote about taking a Bludger to the head two minutes into his first game). This is a new, invented beat that softens Wood into a reassuring figure rather than a driven, slightly terrifying captain.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The invented anecdote replaces Wood's manic competitive drive with gentle reassurance, fundamentally misrepresenting his character. The book's Wood would never open with self-deprecating vulnerability before a match — he would be glaring and punching his fist.

damaging_scenes.scene: Film Scene 8 — Quidditch match action (PS film): Wood knocked out by Bludger
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Wood is an active, competent Keeper throughout the match. He makes saves, is part of the tactical game plan, and is never incapacitated.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: A Slytherin player grabs a Beater's bat and hits a Bludger directly into Wood's stomach, knocking him out and sending him plummeting. He is incapacitated mid-match.
damaging_scenes.explanation: This invented moment undermines Wood's competence as Keeper and captain. It exists for spectacle but damages the portrayal of him as the skilled, reliable backbone of the Gryffindor team.

damaging_scenes.scene: Film Scene 9 (CoS film) — Courtyard confrontation with Slytherin / Malfoy 'Mudblood' scene
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Wood is furious and confrontational with Flint over the pitch booking, 'positively spitting with rage', landing hard in his anger. He is restrained only by the need to keep things clean for Snape's refereeing. His protectiveness of the team and competitive fury are vivid.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Wood appears briefly, reads Snape's note, asks 'You've got a new Seeker? Who?' and then says 'Save it for the match' to hold back Fred and George after the 'Mudblood' slur. He is calm and managerial rather than incandescent.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The book's Wood is barely containable in this scene — his rage at Flint is one of his most vivid moments. The film reduces him to a composed authority figure, losing the comic intensity that defines him.

damaging_scenes.scene: Film Scene 9 (CoS film) — Pre-Hufflepuff match / match cancellation
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Wood's pre-match pep talk fails him entirely — he tries to speak, makes odd gulping noises, and shakes his head hopelessly, beckoning the team to follow. When the match is cancelled he runs toward McGonagall still on his broomstick, devastated, shouting about the cup. His emotional investment is at its peak.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Wood shouts 'Cancelled! They can't cancel Quidditch—' and is cut off by McGonagall. The moment is present but brief, with no build-up of the failed pep talk or the full weight of his devastation.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The failed pep talk is a small but telling character beat — Wood so overwhelmed he literally cannot speak — that is entirely absent. The cancellation reaction is present but compressed.

damaging_scenes.scene: Film Scene 40 / Film Scene 41 — Battle of Hogwarts: Wood carries Colin Creevey / sits with Aberforth
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Wood appears in the Room of Requirement among returning allies, and later carries Colin Creevey's body into the Great Hall in a fireman's lift, telling Neville he can manage alone. It is a brief but dignified, purposeful moment.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: The film shows Wood carrying a boy identified as 'Nigel' (a film-only character) rather than Colin Creevey, and later shows Wood sitting with Aberforth in the Great Hall aftermath. The Colin Creevey moment is reassigned.
damaging_scenes.explanation: Minor reassignment — the body Wood carries is a film-original character rather than Colin, slightly diluting the poignancy of the moment, though Wood's presence and function are otherwise preserved.

damaging_scenes.scene: Film (PoA) — Wood's absence from the Firebolt subplot and dementor conversation
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Wood seeks Harry out specifically to discuss the dementor problem and the Firebolt confiscation. He tells McGonagall he doesn't care if the Firebolt throws Harry as long as he catches the Snitch first, gets yelled at, and reports back sheepishly. This is one of his funniest and most characteristic scenes — his Quidditch obsession overriding all normal human concern.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: This entire subplot — Wood's conversation with Harry about dementors, his disastrous attempt to retrieve the Firebolt from McGonagall, and his report back — is absent from the film.
damaging_scenes.explanation: This is the single funniest and most character-defining Wood scene in the entire book series. His telling McGonagall he doesn't care if the broom kills Harry as long as Harry catches the Snitch first perfectly encapsulates his obsession. Its absence is a significant loss of characterisation.

damaging_scenes.scene: Film (PoA) — Wood's dawn training session and tactical diagrams
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Wood wakes Harry at dawn, delivers a 20-minute tactical presentation with three boards of diagrams and arrows that put the team to sleep, is oblivious to their exhaustion, and suspects Colin Creevey of being a Slytherin spy. His manic energy and comic obliviousness are at full stretch.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: This scene does not appear in the PoA film. Wood's year-long obsessive preparation for the cup is conveyed only through brief references.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The dawn training scene is the fullest expression of Wood's character in PoA — his desperation, his obliviousness, his tactical mania. Its absence means the film Wood never achieves the comic depth the books establish.

damaging_scenes.scene: Film (PoA) — Hufflepuff match: Wood's emotional breakdown and Quidditch Cup win
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Wood is unable to give his pre-match pep talk, has lockjaw shaking Diggory's hand, calls time-out in the storm, is anguished when Harry falls, tries to drown himself in the showers after losing, and then — when Gryffindor finally wins the cup — seizes Harry and sobs unrestrainedly into his shoulder. McGonagall cries harder than Wood. Wood passes Harry the cup weeping.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: The Hufflepuff match in PoA is shown but heavily compressed. Wood's emotional arc — the failed pep talk, the shower breakdown, the cup-winning tears — is largely absent. The cup win is not shown in the film at all (the film ends before the Gryffindor-Slytherin match that wins the cup).
damaging_scenes.explanation: Wood's entire emotional arc across three books — the desperate hunger to win the cup, the repeated failures, the final cathartic victory — culminates in him sobbing on Harry's shoulder. The film never delivers this payoff. This is the most structurally significant damage to the character: his defining goal and its resolution are absent.

damaging_scenes.scene: Film (GoF) — World Cup campsite: Wood's cameo
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Wood drags Harry over to his parents' tent, introduces him excitedly, and tells him he has just been signed to the Puddlemere United reserve team. It is a warm, brief scene showing his life after Hogwarts and his continued affection for Harry.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: This cameo does not appear in the GoF film. Wood is absent from the World Cup sequence entirely.
damaging_scenes.explanation: A small but pleasant continuity beat that confirms Wood's post-Hogwarts success is lost. Minor damage only.
